comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]Midnight is breathing down our necks and so it's time to launch a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions noted below, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after that point will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 341,928th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.2 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. 

Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  I've decided that questions about getting goodies from spirits are also permanently off topic here. The point of occultism is to develop your own capacities, not to try to bully or wheedle other beings into doing things for you. I've discussed this in a post on my blog.

The image? I field a lot of questions about my books these days, so I've decided to do little capsule summaries of them here, one per week.  This is my sixth-third published book, and you'll have a little trouble finding it right now, because its former publisher let it go out of print last year. The upsides are, first, that this gave me the chance to revise, expand, and improve it considerably; second, that I was able to give it the title I originally assigned it, Merlin's Wheel -- did you know that authors have no control over the titles and cover art of their books? -- and third, that I had it placed with a second publisher less than 24 hours after the old one let go of it.

The book -- well, it's a sequel of sorts to my earlier book The Celtic Golden Dawn, providing a set of eight seasonal ceremonies inspired by ancient mystery rites, celebrating the legendary life of Merlin around the cycle of the year as a system of spiritual self-initiation. The new version has been expanded so that it can also be practiced within the ritual system of the Golden Section Fellowship and its related traditions, including the Dolmen Arch system and the Fellowship of the Hermetic Rose. It'll be out later this year, and I'll make an announcement here and on my blog once it's available for preorder.

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:1]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

comments.title: Yoga and mediums
comments.content:
Good morning JMG,

Doing research on some astrological symbols, that I am using for a meditation project, I came upon a yoga archive, that at the time had the best descriptions. But it also had links to other topics in the archive, like karma, reincarnation, and so on.
I got curious and read several of them. And well… I…
hanging around the various ADR and Ecosophia forums. Picking up druidry, books from Dion Fortune, Levi, Hall, Yeats, Blake,… It struck me, how much of it is the same. For instance your Few notes on reincarnation And Dion Fortunes Trough the Gates of death, are complete, well not copies. It is just, that the structure, themes and guidelines discussed are the same. How much did yoga influence the occult thought in the west?

Lately I have been browsing some online posts by people who claim to be mediums. With a thought on the shape of our times and all the fake news, it occurred to me, that it would be prudent to ask about the dangers of listening to supposed mediums. I have cross-checked the claimed with astrology, as far as an amateur is capable. And have asked my Ogham sticks for opinion on the mater; They thought, that the material was good food for thought, but not to relive to much on it. Still some advice and guidelines, from a person of experience, on how to deal with material gotten from mediums would be appreciated.

Best regards,
V

replies.title: Re: Yoga and mediums
replies.content:
1) Western occultists have had some exposure to Hindu ideas of reincarnation over the last century and a half, but there were detailed descriptions of reincarnation in the West before then, and they also said much the same things. I know this is unfashionable these days, but the suggestion I'd make is that these similarities happen because reincarnation is a reality, and when people do enough spiritual practice to get past whatever dogma they've been raised with, that's what they find.

2) Mediums are an incredibly mixed bag. Some are honest, some are as fake as a millionaire's worries about climate change, and it can be very hard to tell the difference. Your Ogham divination seems sensible to me: you can certainly use channeled material as a source of interesting ideas, but don't assume that it's accurate or rely on it in any way.

replies.title: Re: Yoga and mediums
replies.content:
I know this is unfashionable these days, but the suggestion I'd make is that these similarities happen because reincarnation is a reality, and when people do enough spiritual practice to get past whatever dogma they've been raised with, that's what they find.

This comment surprised me--my impression has been that people have been increasingly likely to believe in both karma and reincarnation, and in fact seek to know who they were in previous lives, etc. At least based on my (not personal) experiences, talking about past lives 30 years ago would've caused you to be institutionalized.

replies.title: Re: Yoga and mediums
replies.content: Where's the contradiction? The fact that social pressures against the acceptance of reincarnation have waned with the decline of mainstream Christianity doesn't change the fact that even before this happened, people who meditated in the Western world (using Western forms of meditation) tolerably often ended up noticing that they'd lived before.

replies.title: Re: Yoga and mediums
replies.content: Maybe I am misinterpreting, but I took your comment that you "know this is unfashionable these days" to mean that belief in reincarnation is waning (?)

replies.title: Re: Yoga and mediums
replies.content: No, quite the contrary -- but rationalist materialism is still very deeply rooted in our society.

replies.title: Re: Yoga and mediums
replies.content:
1) It struck me, how I treated my reading of it as an interesting account of the same thing we discuss here. :-)
On a related note; I just went and did a internet search for religions that do not believe in reincarnation. And found … none.
I’ll have to elaborate on this one, will I?
(Please do note, that this are results of an internet research of a complete layperson. Done in 10 minutes)
Naturally Shamanic beliefs and Hinduism & Buddhism are out.
Chinese traditional religions had a concept of “river of oblivion and rebirth”
Now we come to the big 3 of the west:
Judaism does not dispute reincarnation. And it has been a part of judaic mystic traditions from the beginning.
Christianity had reincarnation as part of the original teaching, but it was phased out early on. And rejected officially only recently. One can still find traces of it in the bible. John 9:1. Mathew 17:9
Islam; the Quran directly renounces it. But then there are the Sufis, who like it not to talk about it. And the Isma’ilism branch, who include it.
I have no idea about Shinto, but I do know that modern Japanese spirituality does include it.
I am by no account an encyclopaedia, but to my knowledge that just leaves the materialists.
I shall then conclude that “Belief in reincarnation is inversely proportional with materialistic tendencies.”

2) a) “as fake as a millionaire's worries about climate change“ ha! Good one. I had to laugh
b) Thank you. I will keep building up my own strength. That seems to work the best.

comments.title: Mercury
comments.content:
Hi again JMG,

I would like to follow up on something you wrote on the Ecosophia January Open Post (and no doubt at least a few times in the past). Just to clarify, I was not the initial person who sought your advice:

1) I just started a business and wanted to start it on Wednesday for Mercury, but for a bunch of reasons I wasn't able to do it until Thursday (and I don't remember the actual hour). Because of bureaucracy, I didn't get acknowledgement until Friday. Is there anything I can do now to retroactively improve the potential fortunes of the business, magically speaking?

2) You recommended "Orange candle, hour of Mercury on eight successive Wednesdays, and recite the Orphic hymn to Mercury". I can do the orange candle thing, but am I supposed to use a new orange candle every Wednesday, or is relighting the same one every week just as good? Once the candle has been completely run down, do I just toss it in the garbage?

3) Is there any particular way to close out the candle ritual/recite the hymn? Does the hymn get recited 8 times or just once?

4) Is there anything else I can do beyond the candle lighting and hymn reciting?

5) Let's say my business involves warfare or beauty products. Do I perform some similar ritual on Tuesday (to Mars) or Friday (to Venus) because of their connotation to my products? (LOL--and if so, should divorce lawyers running their own firm make offerings to Mercury, Venus, and Mars?)

6) On a semi- (ok, quarterly-) philosophical note, what effect are we really having on the gods when we devote rituals to them? Presumably, we are pretty insignificant to them, after all...

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Mercury
replies.content:
1) Nope.

2) Depends on the candle. If it's a little candle, use it up. If it's a big candle, snuff it when you're finished with the prayer and relight it again the next week. Yes, the remnants of the candle can be thrown out.

3) By all means come up with something if you want to, but it's not required.

4) Well, what kind of magic do you know what to do?

5) Nope. Business is business, no matter what you sell.

6) You're not having any effect on the gods. You're attuning yourself to the influences that radiate out from them. It's very much as though you were a radio receiver; the receiver has no effect whatsoever on the transmitter, but proper tuning of the receiver allows the signal to be picked up and come through clearly.

replies.title: Re: Mercury
replies.content:
Thanks.

Re: 5) I know no magic. What magic should I learn in order to succeed in my business?

replies.title: Re: Mercury
replies.content: If you want to succeed in business, concentrate on learning about business. If you happen to be interested in magic, and had some skills in it, you might be able to use those skills to further your business -- but if business is your interest, focus on that. One of the great laws of magic is that unity of will is necessary for success in anything. It's by wanting one thing with all your heart, and making other interests secondary to that principal interest, that you can accomplish your dreams.

replies.title: Re: Mercury
replies.content: Thanks.

comments.title: Odd Goings On
comments.content:
Hi Everyone,
I live on a small farm and it is quite private here. We never get people straying into our farm.

I raise rabbits and when they are weaned, I let them out of their cages to run around the barn floor with their siblings. I had to put one bunny in a cage as it is runty and had developed a eye infection. It would be warmer on the barn floor in a huddle of its siblings but I could give it a special diet and medical attention in a cage.

I put it in a cage one evening. The next morning, I came out to tend the rabbits and the little guy was back on the floor with its siblings. Its eye had healed and it looked more energetic.

I examined the cage, which was still closed, and it was in perfect shape and the rabbit could not have escaped. Someone opened the door of the cage and put the rabbit back on the floor and closed the cage door. My husband and I think this was done by magic. Odd things have happened here before involving objects disappearing and sometimes reappearing months later in the same spot they were when they went missing.

I went to put my Bridget doll that I made of sedges (reeds) last year in the fire before I put up the new doll. She spent the whole year on the window ledge by our woodstove and on the first of February, she disappeared. This was a doll about 12 inches high.

In both cases, I felt the intention behind these events was kind. It is still a little odd to me that physical objects and creatures should be moving around like this. Are we looking at an extremely burly house elf or a poltergeist of great power?

Does anyone have any insight into these strange goings on?

Maxine

replies.title: Re: Odd Goings On
replies.content: You might well have a kindly house elf, or some other kind of helpful spirit. Don't thank it, or it will go away.

replies.title: Re: Odd Goings On
replies.content: Not the OP. Why would thanks drive it away?

replies.title: Re: Odd Goings On
replies.content: Nobody knows, but that's a traditional rule all through European fairy lore -- never thank them. It invariably offends them.

replies.title: Re: Odd Goings On
replies.content: Though leaving out a wee dish of milk is sometimes done I believe

replies.title: Re: Odd Goings On
replies.content: Yes, or some other offering. Find out what people used to do in your area, and do that; the local spirits have very long memories.

replies.title: Re: Odd Goings On
replies.content:
Hmm. Similar character traits seem to exist among humans, too. To take myself as an example - if a relation is close enough, say co-workers, I don't value a "thank you" very much. All the "thank you"s in the world won't stop the other one from kicking your ass if it deems "appropriate" and you're vulnerable enough. I learnt that lesson a rather unpleasant way.

I don't know about the vulnerabilities of the spirit beings in question - but judging from what scraps of lore I've heard so far I would be surprised if close coexistence with human beings isn't a very mixed bag for them. Of what worth is a "thank you" if other things are far more important and he who utters those words just displays his ignorance by saying so? "Ah, one of those ignorant thank-you-sayers again. He doesn't know the customs. Better be off for good."

This is of course just a wild guess - and I admit a very embarrassing one as it reminds me of being a thank-you-sayer far too often, despite not wanting to be one.

So today, no thank you! ;-)

Greetings,
Nachtgurke

replies.title: Re: Odd Goings On
replies.content: I read once that in parts of the middle east when you receive a gift or favor you're generally expected to reciprocate. Saying "Thanks" instead signals that you won't or can't return the favor, and can be seen as rude in some contexts. I haven't been able to find any other source corroborating its rudeness, though, so take it with a grain of salt.

replies.title: Re: Odd Goings On
replies.content:
Ah, but one little thing I have to offer - either to you or to anybody who might like it. I love Harp music but unfortunately many tracks you can find sound very much like taken directly from some kind of fantasy game. I'm listening to this one right now while reading and writing and it's pleasant enough. It's called "The Winter Quest of a Druid".

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kyBuDGdPz84

Nachtgurke

replies.title: Re: Odd Goings On
replies.content: (not OP)I find that they appreciate a wry chuckle of recognition ;-)

replies.title: Re: Odd Goings On
replies.content: In the same vein, rather than 'Thank you' I tend to quietly say "That's fun" when I recognise such goings on.

I wondered if this is partly due to an accidental polarity magic working?
So far as I know, she's not a student of magic or other occult topics, so I don't plan to approach her about doing polarity magic workings--

replies.content: Yes, you've got a fine polarity working going on. Remember that most of magic is simply taking something that works in everyday life, and doing it systematically! As long as the two of you avoid getting romantic, you should be fine -- the polarity between the two of you will keep on rising from the material and etheric planes to the astral plane, where it radiates out and charges the workspace with what, back in the day, we called "good vibes."

replies.title: Re: Lesser Polarity Magic
replies.content:
Not the OP, but I suddenly wonder about something from somewhere I worked before. Two of the people working there were very clearly into each other, but one of them was well over a decade older than the other. I think it made them uncomfortable, so they never acted on it; but if both of them were present, then everything went absurdly well for everyone around them. This effect was reliable enough our manager intentionally made sure the two of them had as many shifts together as possible; and when a new manager took over and decided that was a bad idea, everything quite quickly fell apart.

Does it sound like they also created an unintentional polarity working?

replies.title: Re: Lesser Polarity Magic
replies.content: Yes. It's actually quite common.

replies.title: Re: Lesser Polarity Magic
replies.content:
I am also reading _Polarity Magic_ and just read the section on assuming hero forms. Was struck by your commentary on the Arthurian legends. In 1995 Sean Connery and Richard Gere were in _First Knight_ a highly unsuccessful Arthurian film. _Braveheart_ came out the same year and a friend of mine wrote a conference paper on why one film was a great success, and the other was a flop. She concluded that First Knight failed because it was not true to the legend. Instead of Camelot falling, Arthur dies but gives his blessing to Lancelot and Guinevere and they inherit the kingdom. (My take was that terrible acting by Gere contributed as well). People complained that _Braveheart_ was not true to history--however my friend pointed out that it was true to the legend as related by the poet Blind Harry. So, the influence of a well-established legend can outweigh the publicity machine of Hollywood and even the star power of Connery and Gere.

Rita

replies.title: Re: Lesser Polarity Magic

comments.title: Affirmation for worst-case thinking
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

a friend suffers a form of worst-case thinking, in an unfamiliar situation expecting the worst possible outcome and thinking (subconsciously) that by expecting the worst she can keep it from happening. Consciously she knows that she does this and it makes no sense but she still doesn't dare change it, every time the worst not happening reinforcing the "success" of the strategy.

I talked to her about affirmations and she's open to trying it but we're a little stumped about what actually the issue is. "I'm small and insignificant" (to change the future by her expectations) doesn't work as she's neither and doesn't want to be. "My thoughts can't change the future" is also not quite right since they often do (but by other mechanisms) and is negative, as is "the worst generally doesn't happen".

Have you heard of this type of thinking and do you see an affirmation that points in the right direction? Or just another way of approaching the matter?

Thank you for your help!

replies.title: Re: Affirmation for worst-case thinking
replies.content:
Yes, I'm quite familiar with that sort of thinking -- it's a common habit. It's infectious, in an odd way -- notice how all three of the "affirmations" you've given here are actually negations! (Yes, even the first; it's phrased positively but expresses a negation, i.e., not large, not significant.)

The affirmation I'd suggest for your friend is "the best can also happen." Warn her that that's probably going to stir up all kinds of bad memories from childhood, which she should journal about; when she remembers disappointments and miseries, or simply finds herself getting angry or depressed after reciting the affirmation, she can write out what she's feeling. Suggest that she just let it flow onto the page, being careful not to censor or edit anything; that's a good way to release the bitter emotions from those experiences and free up the emotional energy locked in them.

Affirmations can't overcome that kind of knotted emotions from the past, but they can stir them up, so that journaling (and conscious reflection generally) can release them. It's a useful pairing of tools.

replies.title: Re: Affirmation for worst-case thinking
replies.content: Sounds a lot like my mother. If this is any help, she is very lucky in her adult life. The negative thinking seems to reflect early and ancestral experiences, but to have no influence over the current events in her life.

comments.content:
Hello JMG, I have two somewhat scattered questions for you today:
1. in the state between incarnations, the memory of the past life is processed. Are previous lives also taken into account here? So I see the consequences of actions over several lives, along the lines of: I did this and that in my life before last and therefore had to experience this in my last life?
2. you said in a comment that the abolition of the Latin Mass was a nail in the coffin for the Catholic Church, can you explain this further?

Thank you very much!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) That depends on how far along you are in the process of spiritual development. In your early lives, the processing is purely a matter of going over what happened. Later on, as you become more conscious and reflective, understanding begins to dawn, and that brings in the legacies of other lives. Eventually you can survey all your lives -- but at that point, you generally don't need to reincarnate again.

2) It's not the use of Latin so much as a magically effective liturgy. The old liturgies, up to and including the Tridentine mass established at the Council of Trent in 1570, had a great deal of power to them; Dion Fortune called the Mass the most widely practiced ritual of white magic in the world, and she wasn't exaggerating. The Novus Ordo mass put in place in 1969, by contrast, is magically inert, having been designed by Modernist liturgical theorists who knew nothing about the deeper side of ritual and were trying to turn it into a "community meal" rather than anything more serious. That's why sensitive people have noticed all along that where the Novus Ordo mass is celebrated, the church feels like an appliance that's been unplugged, where a church in which one of the older liturgies is celebrated feels like an appliance that's plugged in and running.

comments.title: Golden Section Fellowship

comments.title: Egregores
comments.content: Hi JMG, The term "egregores" has come up repeatedly. I know you recommend a book last week, but is it possible to give a short definition / overview here? I feel a bit lost... even after asking my search engine! Many thanks.

replies.title: Re: Egregores
replies.content: An egregor is an artificial personality created by any group of people caught up in a common set of emotional experiences. Think of the way that every place you've worked and every group you've belonged to has its own "personality," in a certain sense; that's its egregor.

replies.title: Re: Egregores
replies.content:
Super interesting topic. Egregores also come about often when a group does ritual work together, or even when a group does joint meditation work. Every member of the group contributes to the egregore, and the egregore can and does "donate" energies to the individual members. One upshot of this is that a group that really bores in to doing great ritual together can sustain some time where the leadership of the group is quite inept at ritual, or unconcerned with it. Everything will continue to "just work out" for awhile. But it's like drawing money out of a savings account without putting any in; sooner or later it runs out. A major drawback to egregores is that they can cause behavior patterns to "mysteriously" reappear that can be quite destructive. JMG has frequently cited the reoccurring problem of grandiose egotism that always seems to resurface in GD groups that causes them to implode, although, to be fair, a certain amount of that also comes from the type of occult work they are doing.

Jean Dubuis was always dubious about egregores,feeling that the liabilities usually outweighed the benefits, and feeling that a better approach was for students to become strong on their own two feet (or whatever) without the extra energy coming from the group mind which always comes with a consequence, good or bad. That said, lots of people climb up the ladder with the help of the egregores of the groups that instruct them. They then have to be able to graduate, which means ending the dependence on the egregore. Also, being able to do this with minimal incident requires a clear idea of whom you really are and good discernment. But those are alwys requirements on the esoteric path no matter how you approach it.

Hope this was useful and not just a tiresome ramble...

replies.title: Re: Egregores
replies.content: Yes, very useful - thanks to both of you!

This has to do with one of the bastardized forms of thaumaturgy that plays such a large role in the Western World: PR. PR presents itself as moving the Overton Window or Opinion Corridor by shaping public opinion; but the crucial step is not actually shaping public opinion, but the perception of public opinion. Very few people who aren't immediately impacted are going to make a fuss about vaccines being dangerous if they think everyone else thinks of them as necessary for the survival of humanity; and so cultivating that perception can allow PR to work even if it cannot convince people of obvious absurdities. A handful of people fall for the PR completely, and so end up under a spell, in which no one has whatever thoughts that need to be hidden, no matter how much evidence is presented.

This would also explain why it is a commonplace of Western culture for the last century or so that everyone else is insane (because the perception is that almost everyone else agrees with the obvious absurdities that the PR system lost the ability to actually sell people on); it also explains why so many certainties of American culture are suddenly in such flux: as soon as Trump's victories in the presidential elections revealed that a large number of people have such "radical" views, the entire distortion that the PR industry created of public opinon began to fall apart.

Does this analysis sound plausible to you?

comments.title: An interesting effect of an amulet
comments.content:
Over the last year we have used forms of the red bag amulet to good effect as part of a layered 'armoury' in going into an 'unstable' environment where the general atmosphere has been improved and where potential weird behaviour that always materialised before (over years!), did not do so. First time success could perhaps have been chance, second time coincidence but with a third time (Christmas 2024) the needle on the meter was seriously pointing at TSW

It has not been something we've talked about with anyone we know and unexpectedly, an amulet was made over this last weekend and given to someone in great anxiety (panic attack level) and had quite a striking result!
As soon as they put it on, they seemed immediately calmer - almost like a a steel shutter had been brought down (or drawbridge raised) on the emotional crap storm they had been weathering.

There are a number of potential factors that could have played a part, but one of them is that the amulet was made by two people who did different parts of the job but both with a focused intent.

It never occurred to me (fool!) that the fashioning of an amulet could benefit from the two polarities and energies of the makers to make the sum of the parts even more effective than a single person making the thing.

Of course, it may not be that (or just that), perhaps we're just better at it now having used a couple ourselves, but the effect was really quite startling and different to the amulets made before for us.

The person is big into psychology and the like, gave him your description of 'changing consciousness in accordance with will', described the use of the amulet as 'super-psychology' in using material things to help one cause changes in one's own way of thinking - e.g. suggested every time he noticed or thought of the amulet for it to be a prompt for an affirmation (I am emotionally resilient and grounded).

Thing is, affirmations take time to work and this was like a switch was suddenly flipped. Extraordinary to witness and a very pleasing result all around.

replies.title: Re: An interesting effect of an amulet
replies.content: Fascinating! I wonder how much of the anxiety was either (a) a matter of etheric forms he was generating himself through worrying and intensely visualizing the things that made him anxious, or (b) the less pleasant possibility that someone was deliberately sending nasty energies at him.

replies.title: Re: An interesting effect of an amulet
replies.content:
I'm not sure - both are distinct possibilities; but for the second option it may not be deliberate but an example of someone doing something with a natural ability rather than malefic intent (though that cannot be ruled out - think energy vampire).
e.g. sharing a house with someone in their late 70s who is able to throw tantrums the way they did as a toddler, but with a personality that has cultivated that activity over 70 yrs and refined it to an art form; and that the constant refrain after the storm has blown over is that the person doing it has no memory of what the said or did.
Complex.

Mainly it was shocking to see the instant effect of the amulet after it was put on. It was an emergency first aid sort of thing.

replies.title: Re: An interesting effect of an amulet
replies.content:
To interject here, the person in question is my twin and, knowing him as I do, I reckon he's generating most of his panic himself. Saying that, his housemate is certainly being extremely trying at the moment and he is very sensitive to any drama in his life.

Interesting idea to think that a red bag amulet can have an impact on something that a person has generated themselves. For what it's worth, I cut out and sewed the bag, added twin-support to it, the salt and then earthworm put in the bent nail.

replies.title: Re: An interesting effect of an amulet
replies.content: Yes, that's one of the lovely things about the red bag. It clears away any stagnant etheric forms, no matter where they come from.

I wonder what happens if a book goes out of print and the previous publisher doesn't "let it go".

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thanks for this as always.

It all depends on your contract. If you're a writer and have the brains the gods gave goldfish, you insist on a reversion clause in the contract -- i.e., a clause that requires the publisher to revert all rights to you once the book has been out of print for some fixed period of time. I've always insisted on those, which is why I can get the rights back and place them with someone else. If you don't get a reversion clause, a publisher can take your book out of print and then just sit on the rights, and you're screwed. One of the things that makes the big corporate publishers so toxic these days is that they offer "reversion clauses" with various loopholes -- a lot of authors have tried to use the reversion clause and found that the publisher won't return the rights to their books no matter what. My guess is that they're using the notional value of the book rights to play games with the paper value of the company.

Forgot to ask my other polarizing question. You mentioned to avoid virgin goddesses in polarity work. In the Welsh Celtic pantheon that means Olwen, but I remember hearing that Elen may also be a version of Olwen. And Sul was called Sulis Minerva. Minerva is mentioned as one to avoid. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
No, you can polarize with both Olwen and Elen, since they marry in the course of their mythic narratives. By "virgin goddesses" I mean goddesses like Minerva, who are permanently virginal. Sul falls into that category; the Romans recognized that when they equated her with Minerva. Ceridwen does not -- in the myths, she's married and has children, and she's also the original of the Loathly Lady in the Arthurian legends, who turns into a beautiful woman and marries Gawain.

The best way to gauge the potential for polarity work with a deity is to get familiar with his or her myths and legends. If they marry, or have less formal relations, they're appropriate for polarity work, and the more central marriage or mating is in their legends, the more appropriate they are. (Thus Olwen and Elen are both well suited to the work, since in both legends the marriage is the central plot engine.)

Good afternoon and thanks for hosting!

I found a place near me that offers introductory blacksmithing classes in which you learn how to make a basic iron knife. I’m thinking that this would be ideal for use as a magical sword (GD style) since I’d have made it myself. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: What a fine idea! The important thing is to keep your intention in mind while you make the knife. With each blow of the hammer and each other action in the process, focus on the idea that the knife will banish hostile spirits and bring the divine white brilliance to banish the darkness of ignorance and evil.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
All smithcrafts -- blacksmith, whitesmith, locksmith -- have been regarded as (usually beneficial) magical crafts for thousands of years. St Clement, the third Roman Pope after Peter, was a blacksmith by tradition, and in England St Dunstan the blacksmith was legendarily able to overcome and banish Satan by seizing him by his nose with heated blacksmith's tongs.

Tongs are foremost among all the blacksmith's tools. A smith can make all other needed tools once he has a pair of tongs, but blacksmith tradition says that the first smith's tongs were given to the first smith by an angel. Without those tongs, the first smith would have been able to make nothing.

Of course, it takes smithcraft to make a metal knife or sword. There is quite a lot of lore about magical knives and swords, of course, and several good books on this lore and legends. Peter Jennings published Blacksmith Gods in 2013. Frederick W. Robins' book, The Smith: The Traditions and Lore of an Ancient Craft (1953) was hard to find, last I looked, but quite readable. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
As I've had occasion to say many times before, the best way to misinterpret a geomantic chart is to overthink it. That's what's going on here.

1) In terms of your judge and witnesses, you have two negative figures and one positive figure. That's a "no." Fortuna Major indicates that something else will help, but you haven't gotten to it yet.

2) In terms of the house charts, a yes or no question is settled by the presence or absence of a mode of perfection. Illness is a 6th house matter, and no mode of perfection connects Puella in the 1st to Rubeus in the 6th. The 1st doesn't connect to anything else in the chart, btw. Here again, the answer is no, the supplement will not help.

Rubeus in the 6th, btw, indicates that you may not be right about the nature of the condition, but you'll need to do more readings to sort that out.

I'm sorry to be the bearer of bad news, but there it is -- I recommend getting a second opinion on the condition, which may not be what you think it is, and looking for other remedies.

comments.title: Would a group manifestation be appropriate now?
comments.content:
Hi John,

Two of my favorite words:

Zeitgeist: "is the way of thinking and feeling (mentality) of an age or certain time-frame. In occult terms a Zeitgeist is an etheric storm, to use Dion Fortune’s definition, with a very strong morphogenetic force that afflicts most people psychologically and can lead to both a period of prosperity and/or enlightenment or collective insanity and decay, such as is the case with a period of war, religious, political or economic hysteria, witch-hunts (old or modern) or crisis, which are all mass-psychoses." https://vamzzz.com/blog/zeitgeist/

Egregore: "In magical and other occult traditions, it is typically seen as having an independent existence, but in other kinds of esotericism, it is merely the collective mind of a religious community, either esoteric or exoteric. In the latter sense, as a collective mind, the term collective entity, preferred by René Guénon, is synonymous with egregore.[1] See the usage overview below. In the apocryphal Book of Enoch, the term had referred to angelic beings known as watchers,[4][5] and it was also used by associated (Enochian) traditions to refer to the specific rituals and practices associated with these entities.[6]" https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Egregore

A lifetime's worth of things have actualized in the last year, certainly the last five, and arguably a decades's worth in the last 14 days. Are we far enough past the storm center, yet, that we can relax a bit and maybe turn more positive? (Speaking for myself, I did a lot of praying last year -- half of it that the world somehow, miraculously, escape the worst of the darkness -- and half that I, and those I cared most about, would get to "Noah's Ark" before it left dock.

Is it safe to come out yet? And, if so, would you consider doing a post, or series of posts, on how to view where we are (in broad strokes, and considering that descent is a long path) and, perhaps, lead a group "manifest" to a brighter future from here?

I know there is a thing about not talking too much about what we are about. But I would imagine something could be woven together, in a broader sense, for those who would like to participate? I like to think I did my small part with https://www.ecosophia.net/a-primer-of-magical-combat/

replies.title: Re: Would a group manifestation be appropriate now?
replies.content:
No, not at all. One of the most important lessons we have to learn is that the age of Pisces, the age of mass consciousness, of prophets leading crowds toward this or that mirage of imminent salvation, is over. The age we are in now, the age of Aquarius, is the age of the individual, in which the spirit is being poured out over everyone and each of us gets to find our own way up the mountain -- if it's even the same mountain we're all on. (My guess is that it's not.)

There is no Noah's Ark, no escape hatch from history, no grand march toward the one true shining tomorrow. We are all wandering in the forest of time, and as the braying of trumpets and the bellowing of leaders fades out behind us, a hush is beginning to gather. In that hush, the thing that can lead each of us, or all of us, or all of us who want to, toward something better than we've got now is to learn to think our own thoughts -- not to copy anyone else's, no matter how impressively the latter may be presented or how good the cause seems.

THINK FOR YOURSELF, SCHMUCK! is the inscription written in fire on the mountain cliff ahead. And with that, I'll wind up this little sermon, and suggest that praying all by yourself, to whatever aspect of the divine calls most to you, is also a good idea just now.

For a few weeks now, I have been regularly praying everyday over the Ecosophian Prayer List that Quin manages and as I do so, I keep wondering if I have the proper effective attitude, etc. So today, I went over some of your various responses to Qs about prayer here on MM or Open Post that I have copied over the years and put together the following for use with Quin's prayer list:

Prayer is not about making one feel powerful. Prayer is about acknowledging that there are beings in the cosmos far greater than us.

Settle into a stable position, turn attention toward the deity or deities to pray to, and talk. Do it as though I have the chance to talk to a wise and powerful elder. Explain why I want to pray to the deity/deities, ask for the outcome.

Imagine energies of blessing, healing, and peace streaming out from me for the people of each prayer request.

At the end, acknowledge "Thy will be done" and thank the deity/deities for listening. (Don't grovel, don't flatter, don't whine.)

Then, at the end of the prayer session, and this is crucial, be silent for a while. Listen for any answers. We may not get any; an answer may be felt -- an emotional tone, say -- or words may surface in the mind; but it's at least as important to listen to deities as it is to speak to them.

Does this seem like an appropriate approach for the prayer list or prayer in general? Any modifications?

Thank you,

WILL1000

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's a very good way to do it. It's fairly close to the way I pray, if that's anything to go by.

comments.title: Magic & Myth
comments.content:
Last week I had a specific fear hanging onto me. Mentally I noted that I had already released the personal hangups that had originally attached me to this fear. It thus didn't make sense that it was still hanging around.

The day before, I'd visited a statue of Apollo and told him about my transformation last year, promising another transformation this year.

Just as I had the thought that I wanted the fear gone, and I'd already released my own attachments to it, the idea popped into my mind that I could slay it like Apollo did the python.

Immediately in my mind's eye the python was right next to me, its huge maw open about to eat me. In the physical world I pantomimed drawing the bow and arrow. In my imagination I had a Studio Ghibli-style moment where my hair floated and I let out a silent scream. I let the arrow loose and the python exploded, imaginary blood all over the room.

The scene dissolved and I was baffled to realize the fear was gone. It hasn't returned since. I thanked Apollo and made sure to not summon the fear again.

1. This sounds like the very definition of magic! Right?

2. Is this a common way that mages use myth for magic, and I just didn't know? Thanks for your time.

replies.title: Re: Magic & Myth
replies.content:
1) Yes, but it's a specific kind of magic, one that worked the way it did because you'd made the contact with Apollo. You might consider getting a small statue of Apollo and burning a little incense to it now and again, partly as a thank you, partly to strengthen the contact.

2) Some do, some don't. It depends on one's relationship with the gods.

I've had a busy day, and am running out of time, so I just thought I'd thank whoever asked the question last week about using both charts for Geomantic divination. Since reading the answer, I've switched to using mostly the House chart with "how will my day go?" or similar as the question, though there's the occasional "will the kids and I end up going out for sushi tonight?" thrown in (the sushi place is expensive), and it has two effects that I'm quite liking:

  2. It's forcing me to become more comfortable with the meanings of all the houses. Up until now, I'd known the meaning of a couple of them (around relationships and work, because they're what I've asked about) but now I'm digging in more.

More observation than question. As I work my way through OPH, I'm at the section on the value of occult training as it relates to where one is on the evolutionary scale. First, I found myself thinking quite a bit during my meditations about the possibility that I'm not actually getting anything out of it, because I'm not as evolved as one should be to work on occult topics. Or, alternatively, am I just at the cusp, which is why I've been interested in these topics my whole life, but the practice has only just "stuck" in my middle-years. Also, there was a line that occult training is "a narrow path set apart for a few" which really got me thinking, on the one hand, do I really want to be associated with the people who first thought up such an elitist idea, and then, well, is it really elitist? To use an analogy, "there are very few people who can solve differential equations in their head" is, I assume (not being such a person), simply true, though reading it the right way certainly sounds just as elitist.

I'll be going through and reading the questions and answers tomorrow.

Happy Monday, and thanks again for hosting,

The Dilettante Polymath

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: What sets occultism apart, and makes it for the few, is simply that most of us have other interests -- and that's wholly appropriate. For most of the lives we spend as human beings, it's far more useful for us to plunge into the various dimensions of human life, to learn from our successes and failures, and to come to know ourselves in this way. Only those who are wrapping up their time in human incarnation will be drawn to occultism, and so by definition they're odd ducks. The crucial point here is that it's a waste of time to proselytize for occultism; it's not for everyone, nor should it be, but anyone who feels called to it and is willing to accept the necessary requirements and put in the hard work that's needed is welcome on the Path.

comments.title: magic to make yourself more lovable
comments.content: in one of your really old posts you mentioned a way of using magic to make yourself more lovable, how do I do this? I've been reading your rituals of high magic series, I hope the answer you give me is specific and actionable

replies.title: Re: magic to make yourself more lovable
replies.content:
It's quite simple, but it does require you to have a good working knowledge of some system of ceremonial magic -- for example, the Golden Dawn system, which I'll use as an example. (If you practice some other system of magic, adapt the following to that.) First, you'll need to do a series of meditations and Pathworkings focusing on Venus and its corresponding sphere on the Tree of Life, Netzach, to activate those influences in your sphere of sensation. Meanwhile you can begin practical work by adding to your daily Middle Pillar exercise the work Israel Regardie included in the last section of his book The Art of True Healing, charging your sphere of sensation with the energies of Netzach. All this is preparation, though it can have a considerable effect.

When you've finished the meditations and pathworkings -- let's say, seven weeks of those (7 x 7, 7 being the number of Netzach), you create and consecrate a talisman of Netzach for the purpose of removing the unlovable aspects from your personality, and therefore making you more lovable. Then you let the talisman work. That'll likely involve some emotional upset as it forces you to confront and let go of the unlovable aspects of your personality, but on the far side of it you'll have become much more lovable and people will treat you accordingly -- for example, by falling in love with you.

How's that for specific and actionable? If you need the details of the Golden Dawn approach, you can look up all of this in my books Paths of Wisdom and Circles of Power. If you're not to that level yet in Golden Dawn studies, any standard introductory volume of GD magic, such as my book Learning Ritual Magic, will teach you everything you need to know.

comments.title: The Devil Himself
comments.content:
Satan is usually depicted as a red, satyr-like being, with horns, hoofs, and tail. There seems to be a connection with fauns/satyrs and Dionysus, particularly the excesses of Bacchanalian pagan festivals. This also fits a larger pattern, in which the pagan elements that couldn't be synthesized ended up demonized (sometimes literally).
However, I'm wondering if there's more to the story -- are there occult reasons/history for this portrayal? I recall reading a book on occultism as a kid, which had an illustration of an entity with a goat head & legs, with women's breasts -- I'm sure you know what I mean. I assume there's a connection there?

replies.title: Re: The Devil Himself
replies.content:
Presumably this is the image you have in mind:
[IMAGE:0]
That's Eliphas Lévi's drawing of Baphomet, the supposed idol of the Templars. Lévi had very specific things in mind when he drew that, about which the people using the image these days have less than no clue.

The conventional image of Satan is borrowed very nearly lock, stock, and barrel from the Greek Pagan image of Pan. There's a reason for that, of course, which is that Pan sums up all the aspects of the Divine that couldn't be included in the Christian understanding of divinity, and so became the Shadow (in the full Jungian sense) of the Christian image of God. Jung had a lot to say about that, and about the psychological cost of the Christian castration of the Divine Image -- you'll find it, if you're interested, in his book Aion and elsewhere. The Fisher King in the Grail legend is another symbolic expression of the same insight, associated with fish, wounded in the "thighs," and unable either to fully live or fully die until someone asks the right question...

comments.title: Radionics cleansing
comments.content:
I read an account of you and Joseph Max using a Hieronymus machine in a group ritual before to progressively cleanse the chakras.

Would it be advisable to adapt this to the equivalent of a daily ritual? So doing the same thing, but with a sample of myself in the well, and going through the chakras? Or do you think another ritual would be better.

Thanks

replies.title: Re: Radionics cleansing
replies.content:
Yes, you can certainly do that. Here's what I'd suggest.

First, use the basic white light rate, 12-22.

Then work through the chakras, starting with the root chakra and going up one at a time to the crown. Make sure each one is clear before going onto the next.

Then -- I presume you have the Hieronymus rate book -- check each of the D.F. ("dark forces") rates listed on page G-31 of the rate book. Make sure all of them are below 50; treat any of them that are above that intensity.

Then finish with another round of 12-22.

That will do the same things as a daily banishing ritual and invocation of positive energies.

comments.title: R.J. Stewart Books
comments.content:
No question, just something to pass along.

If anyone is looking for books by R.J. Stewart, do *not* buy them from the slimy South American river or any of the other usual suspects online. Evidently most or all his books are out of print, so naturally they're being sold as the rarities they now are for the typical 2, 3 or 4 times the original price. I was about to shell out 46 bucks (plus shipping) for a used copy of The Underworld Initiation when on a whim I went over to rjstewart.net and was able to buy it for 20 dollars, shipping included, and it arrived promptly 4 days later. I'm guessing maybe Robert and his people have a small stash of copies stockpiled are are still making them available for the original price. Whatever the explanation, I would heartily suggest that if you're interested in any of his books, head over to his website and take advantage of his offering--no telling how much longer it will last! (I did have to create a PayPal account, so take that into consideration.)

comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I ran across something interesting in my Lullian Art research that I was hoping you might know about.

Elemental theory was a major part of Llull's work, especially the earlier versions of the Art. In addition to the standard scheme of air=hot, moist; fire=hot, dry; etc., Llull also had the idea of proper and appropriated traits.

So air has moisture as its proper trait but it appropriated heat from fire; water properly cold and appropriates moisture from air; and so on.

My question is, have you run across this scheme of proper and appropriated traits of the elements? If so, are you aware of any sources that are earlier than Llull (second half of 13th century, roughly)? And also are there any more recent traditions that use it?

Sorry for running up to the wire on this one. I appreciate any information you can provide!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Yes, I've encountered it before in medieval and Renaissance sources. If I recall correctly, it's ancient -- you'll want to check Galen and Avicenna, the two main sources from which the European Middle Ages drew their elemental theory.
